name: Rubber
crop_cycle_note: Perennial tree; immature (pre-tapping) phase 6-7 years, then tapped for latex for 25-30 years

growth_stages.name: Nursery / Seedling
characteristics: Green budded or seedling stock, height under 1 m
care_focus: Protect from leaf fungal disease in nursery beds

growth_stages.name: Immature (Pre-Tapping)
characteristics: Trunk girth increasing but below tappable girth (~50 cm at 125 cm height)
care_focus: Regular fertilizer application per age-based schedule
Cover cropping (legumes) to improve soil and control weeds
Protect from grazing and fire in young plantations

growth_stages.name: Tapping-Ready / Mature
characteristics: Tapping panel opened, regular latex flow begins
care_focus: Follow correct tapping frequency (e.g. alternate day) to avoid over-exploitation
Monitor tapping panel for tapping panel dryness (TPD) or disease entry

growth_stages.name: Wintering / Refoliation
characteristics: Leaf fall (wintering) followed by flushing of new leaves
Tapping typically rested or reduced during this period
care_focus: Rest tapping during peak leaf fall to conserve tree health
Watch new flush for powdery mildew infection risk

growth_stages.name: Peak Yield Phase
characteristics: Maximum latex yield period with mature, healthy canopy
care_focus: Maintain balanced fertilization to sustain yield
Rain guarding during monsoon to protect tapping panel and sustain tapping days

growth_stages.name: Old / Senile
characteristics: Declining latex yield, panel exhaustion from repeated tapping
care_focus: Consider timber harvest value at replanting

operations: Wintering/refoliation period tapering off, new leaf flush strengthens
Apply summer manuring dose once new leaves mature
Prepare rain guards before monsoon tapping resumes
precautions: Watch new flush closely for powdery mildew during this tender-leaf period
Avoid tapping stress during active leaf flushing

operations: Install rain guards on tapping panels to allow continued tapping
Apply fungicide spray schedule against abnormal leaf fall disease
Monitor drainage in low-lying plantation areas
precautions: Peak risk period for abnormal leaf fall (Phytophthora) and Corynespora leaf fall
Tapping panel dryness risk increases with over-tapping in wet conditions

operations: Remove rain guards gradually as rains reduce
precautions: Residual humidity can still favor fungal leaf disease - continue monitoring

operations: Reduce or rest tapping as trees shed leaves
precautions: Avoid tapping stressed trees during peak leaf fall to protect long-term yield

diseases.name: Abnormal Leaf Fall Disease
symptoms: Water-soaked black lesions on petiole causing rapid leaf drop, often with the leaflet still green
Pod rot and twig dieback can accompany severe infection
Rapid defoliation during continuous monsoon rain
treatment: Aerial spraying of copper-based fungicide (e.g. 1% Bordeaux mixture) during monsoon
Ground spraying on young plants where aerial spray isn't feasible
Clear fallen infected leaves to reduce inoculum
prevention: Follow the recommended prophylactic spraying calendar before/during monsoon

diseases.name: Powdery Mildew
symptoms: White powdery fungal growth on young, tender leaves
Affected young leaves shrivel and drop, delaying canopy formation
Most damaging during the wintering/refoliation flush
treatment: Dust with fine sulphur powder during early morning hours
Spray a recommended systemic fungicide if dusting isn't sufficient
prevention: Time sulphur dusting to coincide with the new leaf flush period
Avoid excess shade that keeps foliage persistently moist

diseases.name: Corynespora Leaf Fall
symptoms: Fishbone-pattern necrotic lesions along the leaf veins
Yellowing followed by defoliation, can occur outside monsoon season too
treatment: Spray a recommended fungicide effective against Corynespora on affected clones
Avoid planting highly susceptible clones in disease-prone areas
prevention: Select tolerant clones where the disease is historically severe
Maintain plant vigor through balanced nutrition

diseases.name: Pink Disease
symptoms: Pinkish, silky fungal growth encircling branches or the upper trunk
Affected branch above the infection point wilts and dies back
Most common on branches in high-canopy, humid areas
treatment: Scrape off the fungal growth and apply Bordeaux paste to the wound
Prune and destroy severely affected branches
prevention: Prune to maintain airflow in dense canopy areas
Regular field inspection during monsoon for early detection

diseases.name: Tapping Panel Dryness (TPD)
symptoms: Partial or complete cessation of latex flow from the tapping cut
Bark in the tapped panel appears dry, sometimes cracked
treatment: Rest the affected panel from tapping to allow recovery
Apply recommended panel-recovery treatments as advised by extension officers
prevention: Follow correct tapping frequency and depth guidelines, avoid over-exploitation
Ensure adequate tree nutrition to support latex regeneration

pests.name: Scale Insects
symptoms: Small brown/grey scale colonies on stems and leaves
Yellowing and weakening of affected shoots
management: Spray neem oil or a recommended contact insecticide on colonies
Prune heavily infested twigs and destroy them

pests.name: Rubber Mites
symptoms: Bronzing/rust-colored discoloration on the leaf undersurface
Premature leaf drop in heavy infestation
management: Spray wettable sulphur or a recommended miticide
Ensure adequate irrigation to reduce plant stress in dry season

pests.name: Termites
symptoms: Mud tunnels/galleries on the trunk surface
Hollowing of stem/root collar in young plants leading to wilting
management: Apply a recommended soil insecticide treatment around affected plant bases
Remove decaying stumps/wood nearby that attract termite colonies

pests.name: Bark-Eating Caterpillar
symptoms: Silken webbed tunnels/galleries visible on the trunk bark
Bark damage weakening the tree over time
management: Manually remove and destroy webbing and larvae found in tunnels
Apply a recommended insecticide paste into the tunnel openings

risk_conditions.impact: High abnormal leaf fall risk, reduced tapping days without rain guards
Moisture stress in young plants, higher mite incidence
Reduced latex flow and increased tapping panel stress
Branch breakage risk in mature trees with heavy canopy
